dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have trouble breathing that started last night and just keeps getting worse.
I see. Are you experiencing any chest pain along with the shortness of breath?
No chest pain, just really hard to breathe.
Can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
My name is Sonia and we are in [CITY].
What day is today?
Hmm, I think it's the 5th.
What happened to you today?
I was short of breath and needed to call 911.
Thank you. You seem alert and oriented. We'll be gentle with everything we do.
I'm going to check your airway. Can I do that?
Yes, sure.
We can hear wheezing on expiration.
How is your breathing right now, comfortable or labored?
It feels stressful. I'm expelling air in whistling sounds.
You have pursed lips, and I can see signs of labored breathing.
Let's listen to your lungs. You have wheezing and rhonchi on both sides on expiration.
I'll now check your oxygen saturation and pulse.
Your oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
We'll check your blood sugar levels now.
The reading is 0, but that's not critical in your current condition and consistent with trends.
Your heart rate is 119 now.
You are breathing 20 times per minute.
Your pulse is strong.
Your blood pressure is 151 over 43.
Your heart rate is still elevated, so let's understand more about your situation.
I see that this might be an asthma flare and maybe some bronchitis. What have you done for relief so far?
I tried using my inhaler, but I ran out last week and haven't had one.
I see. Do you have a history of asthma or other breathing conditions?
Yes, I have asthma. It's mild, but I do go to the hospital when it's severe.
Got it. Do you also have any other medical conditions, like high blood pressure or kidney issues?
I have high blood pressure and stage 5 kidney disease. I'm dependent on renal dialysis.
I was told you found yourself sitting on the couch in the living room when we arrived. Is that correct?
Yes, that's where I am now.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how challenging is your breathing right now?
I don't feel pain, but my breathing is so hard I'd say it's a 10.
Let me recheck your vital signs to confirm.
Pulse remains 119, respiration 20, and blood pressure 151/43.
Oxygen saturation is still 99%, and the EKG still shows sinus tachycardia.
We'll be using a nebulizer with Albuterol and Atrovent to help open your airways.
We'll also start an IV and give a dose of Dexamethasone to reduce airway inflammation.
IV access is now established in your left wrist, just as documented in the procedure at 22:27 in the notes.
The EKG is ready and shows sinus tachycardia.
We're doing a quick secondary assessment now. Are you okay with that?
Yes, go ahead.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-A&O x4.
You are conscious and well-oriented with no signs of neurological imbalance.
ECG monitor has been placed and is capturing a normal sinus tachycardia rhythm.
Watching vitals after treatment starts.
Your pulse has dropped slightly to 101.
Respiratory rate increased to 30 per minute.
Blood pressure is 124 over 65 now.
Oxygen remains at 99% and EKG still shows sinus tachycardia.
The IV is secure. Dexamethasone has already been given per protocol.
I also noted you take medications like amlodipine (Lotrel), carvedilol (Coreg), and hydralazine for hypertension.
You also didn't mention any allergy to Flagyl, but your chart says you should avoid it. Just helpful if we need to adjust meds.
Based on your vital signs and condition, we're following the medical protocol for asthma exacerbation.
Where would you like to go for further treatment?
[COMPANY_NAME].
Understood. We'll prepare for transport there now.
Would you be comfortable for us to prepare the stretcher and move you onto it now?
Yes, I'm ready.
Then let's get you situated. Ready, count of three: One, two, three.
You're all packaged and secured. On our way now to [COMPANY_NAME].
We're monitoring you continuously. You're currently receiving treatment per protocol and are stable.
Dexamethasone was given earlier, and we've stabilized for travel.
I'll make sure the hospital knows of your A&O x4 status, GCS of 15, and labored breathing from your EKG.
Your vital signs were 119 pulse, 20 respirations, and 151/43 blood pressure - we consider those vitals (V/S) and will provide a full picture in our report.
You don't appear to have any pain based on your breathing complaints, and this was documented in the pain assessment as 0.
Your kidney disease, current renal dialysis, and ongoing high blood pressure are part of the report too.
I'll highlight your use of Amlodipine, Carvedilol, and Hydralazine as part of your medical history.
Just to confirm, no Flagyl allergies.
My report includes all of that, along with your GCS score and current management plan.
The IV was started in an extremity vein in the left wrist at 22:27, an important detail we've captured.
We administered Ipratropium at 22:21 as part of our care. You seemed to improve afterward.
We're transporting you with the highest priority for hospital care. You'll be handed over in room 16 ready for inpatient recovery.
We will give a full report to the receiving RN there, just as per your request.
Our front lights are on for fast transport.
The ambulance is ready with all equipment.
I have final vitals just confirmed at the 22:43 clock read. Are you okay with monitoring?
Another EKG read to confirm sinus tachycardia. Just confirming your rhythm during the transit.
Rhythm maintains. Returned 22:43 data. We'll keep that current.
Final pulse and resps: 101, 30 - pulse improved while resps show difficulty catching up for the last hour.
No new interventions at this point, all durable for the hard transport.
Time to roll in seconds. Let's execute on that once it's clear.
We're transporting for any acute exacerbation. The plan will be continued there without delay.
We have a clear and uneventful call.
No hiccups in this transfer from point A to B.
Everything has been covered in this CRM and will assist the hospital.
I just wanted to touch on how you're doing at this moment putting it onto stretcher.
Stable, no worsening symptoms - just wanted to confirm on our progress.
Let me and my partner go ahead with some last-minute array checks and road.
Any final questions for us before starting?
No, you all look prepared.
Then we can go exact now.
Alright, let's make tracks! Thank you for giving me and the team the trust today.
Take care, and good luck at the hospital.
We'll be with you this whole way, I promise.
No debrief is overrated. Even with a clear case, the skills stay strong and mindset right every time.
Let's under-pressure this move and get her all set real soon.
Be back for any critical message or stability check if we so require.
Nailed it on the protocol, on the action, and on the rescue.
Location now locked and secured for acute observation.
One more final stability round and we will leave accordingly.
Our gear is clean, patient is clean, and breathing options are adequate for arrival.
Competent, stable, and no further messages expected from the field today. Let it pass up and on into the next sure.
Let the next team know it's a cold case with full yard clearance. Just prompt the hospital director, done.
Then the last possible value is handed over directly and then we can officially go.
Again, the GCS 15, maybe overtime it would help the best in asthma control if the wheezing still persists.
Beyond that and beyond what we've already crossed here.
